The surgeon removes medial attachments to access the plane between the posterior aspect of the gallbladder and the liver, revealing the anatomical separation between the gallbladder wall and the liver tissue.

The surgeon dissects along the back wall of the gallbladder, approaching the cystic duct. A large vessel is visible, identified as the closest structure resembling a cystic artery, located posteriorly. No anterior artery is found.

The surgeon observes the necrotic appearance of the gallbladder, suggesting possible thrombosis, and proceeds to taper down towards the cystic duct. Collaborating with a colleague, the decision is made to amputate the necrotic tissue.
The surgeon exposes the cystic duct, revealing bile emerging from it, surrounded by a thick layer of adventitial tissue.
Bile is visibly flowing from the cystic duct, indicating no distal obstruction. The surgeon clearly exposes the lumen and begins oversewing it using a PDS suture.
The surgeon observes the open cystic duct, visualizing the ICG dye within it. The common bile duct is visible to the patient's left, aiding in anatomical orientation.

Obviously, I think it was very helpful to have as well to know that up above that there were no other injuries. If I had injured a bile duct up above, we should definitely be seeing ICG flowing and we don't.
The surgeon runs a suture back up along the previously oversewn area, preparing to tie it together to secure the closure.
The surgeon removes debris from the gallbladder, focusing on debulking necrotic tissue. The top half of the gallbladder mucosa is cauterized to manage tissue integrity.

But in the end, I was very pleased with how this looked. I'm not really sure if there's anything else I could have done. At least in my comfort level and skill set. Compressing on the repair to see if I get any bile to flow out and I didn't. So I knew that I had a nice closure.

The surgeon carefully removes remaining debris and necrotic tissue from the surgical site, ensuring no remnants are left behind.

Reflecting on the case, it just reminds me why I absolutely despise this organ and this operation. I was very surprised to find a necrotic gallbladder that had a drain in it in a patient who otherwise was convalescing and being optimized for cholecystectomy before they developed the cholangitis.
I had not seen that necessarily before, so I was surprised.
It just reminds us that we always have to be cognizant when we do these cases because it is never just a wall bed. So final look of things.
